title: From-Scratch Machine Learning in Python with NumPy

added_topics: Connecting the mathematical regression definition to Python code

selected_videos.title: Implementing Gradient Descent For Simple Linear Regression In Python / NumPy | ML Course 2.45

selected_videos.inclusion_reason: The title specifically identifies gradient descent, simple linear regression, Python, and NumPy. This directly fills the optimization and parameter-update gap left uncertain by the broader linear-regression foundation.

selected_videos.title: Linear Regression From Scratch in Python (Mathematical, Closed-Form)

selected_videos.inclusion_reason: The title explicitly identifies a mathematical, from-scratch closed-form linear-regression implementation. It is included as a distinct comparison with gradient-descent training rather than as another general regression introduction; the exact NumPy and workflow coverage should be verified.

selected_videos.title: Implementation of Logistic Regression using Numpy Python  |  Open Knowledge Share
selected_videos.inclusion_reason: The title explicitly identifies logistic regression implemented with NumPy, making it the strongest available candidate for extending the curriculum from regression to classification. Coverage of sigmoid, log loss, gradients, probability predictions, and training-loop details remains provisional.

covered_topics: Gradient descent implementation for linear regression
Python project workflow for a predictive model
uncovered_topics: Reusable model APIs with explicit fit, predict, and predict_proba methods
Explicit confirmation of sigmoid, log loss, logistic-regression gradients, and probability predictions
Leakage-safe train-test splitting and identical feature scaling for training and test data
Configurable training loops with convergence tracking
Systematic MSE, RMSE, accuracy, precision, recall, and log-loss implementation
Finite-difference gradient checks, invariant checks, and debugging NumPy shape, broadcasting, NaN, and exploding-loss errors
Reproducible experiment organization and explicit comparison between from-scratch and scikit-learn models
limitations: The selected set was checked against the supplied preferred count and duration range and does not exceed the hard time budget.
All candidates rely on metadata-only evidence, so mathematical explanations, code depth, preprocessing details, testing, and scikit-learn comparisons should be verified while watching.

Candidate 14 supplies the clearest real-dataset workflow signal, but its title does not confirm that the classifier is implemented from scratch; it should supplement rather than replace the manual NumPy implementation.
The pool lacks a clearly identified resource dedicated to numerical testing, debugging, reusable model APIs, and comprehensive metrics.
